summary: Patrick McKenzie announces Starfighter, a company cofounded with Thomas and Erin Ptacek that will publish free-to-play Capture The Flag programming games designed to teach rare programming skills and serve as work-sample tests for hiring. The business model is contingency recruiting: companies pay Starfighter a fee when they hire players who demonstrate exceptional skill through the games. McKenzie argues that traditional tech hiring is fundamentally broken, filtering on resumes and keywords rather than ability, and structurally excluding qualified candidates. Starfighter aims to replace this with direct skill measurement, connecting top-performing players with CTOs rather than HR inbox black holes. The post also notes McKenzie is selling Bingo Card Creator and transitioning away from self-employment to focus on Starfighter full-time.
key_points: Starfighter CTFs are free work-sample tests that teach rare programming skills while simultaneously assessing candidates for employers

The tech industry's hiring process is structurally broken, filtering on credentials and keywords rather than demonstrated ability, which excludes qualified candidates including those from underrepresented backgrounds
The business model is contingency recruiting — companies pay a percentage of first-year salary when they hire Starfighter-sourced candidates, so players never pay
The only way to 'game' the assessment is to actually learn the skills employers want, which Starfighter considers an epic success rather than a problem
McKenzie is leaving self-employment and selling Bingo Card Creator to focus on Starfighter full-time
money_quotes: We're not here to fix the technical interview: we're here to destroy it, and create something new and better in its place.
You will code to play. You will not pay to code.
The technology industry is fundamentally unserious as to how it presently identifies and employs engineers.
Donald Knuth would be selected out of the hiring process for senior C programmer with Unix experience before any human had ever considered him at most tech companies.
The technology industry has, through neglectfully embracing hiring policies which are so irrational as to shock the conscience, created a gigantic mountain of free money.
Starfighter claims that lonely mountain as its birthright. We are going to slay the dragons guarding it and then strip-mine it.
We come here not to serve technology recruiters, but instead to replace them with a small shell script.
Are we worried that players will teach themselves these skills just to play Starfighter? We're counting on it.
key_insight: The tech industry's reliance on resume filtering and whiteboard interviews over work-sample skill assessment represents both a massive market inefficiency and a structural barrier to entry that a well-designed CTF platform can profitably dismantle.
